title: scenario_search_compromised_floor
description: Search and rescue in a structure where floor integrity is compromised by fire damage

timeline.title: Dispatch
timeline.description: Structure fire in a two-story Victorian home with reported occupant on second floor. Building is over 100 years old with wood frame construction.

timeline.title: On Scene
timeline.description: Fire visible on first floor rear. Heavy smoke from second floor. Age of structure and fire location raise floor integrity concerns.

timeline.title: Entry
timeline.description: Search crew enters. First-floor hallway boards show charring. IC warns of potential floor failure on first-floor-fire path.

timeline.title: Floor Warning
timeline.description: Crew hears cracking under foot near the kitchen entrance. IC orders caution.

timeline.title: Victim Located
timeline.description: Victim found on second floor. Return path shows partial floor compromise on the staircase landing.

timeline.title: Rescue Complete
timeline.description: Victim removed via exterior ladder. All crew accounted for. Structure transitioned to defensive.

node-1.title: Assessing Floor Integrity Before Entry
node-1.situation: You arrive at a century-old two-story wood-frame Victorian home. Fire is active on the first floor rear. A resident reports their parent is on the second floor and cannot self-evacuate. The construction type and fire location raise serious floor integrity concerns for the first floor and staircase.
node-1.question: How do you manage the floor integrity risk during search entry?

options.label: A
options.text: Brief crew on floor failure indicators — spongy feeling, cracking sounds, smoke from floor seams. Advance by hugging the walls where joists are strongest, test each step before committing full weight, and identify a secondary egress via exterior ladders before entry.

options.label: B
options.text: Move quickly — hesitation increases exposure time and puts the victim at greater risk.

options.label: C
options.text: Do not enter — the floor integrity risk makes interior operations unacceptable. Search from exterior only.

node-2.title: Spongy Floor Detected
node-2.situation: Your crew is advancing along the right wall of the first-floor hallway toward the staircase. One firefighter reports the floor feels spongy and soft underfoot near the kitchen entrance — a sign the floor joist below may be compromised. The staircase is just beyond this section.
node-2.question: How do you navigate the compromised floor section?

options.label: A
options.text: Stop movement. Re-route via the wall opposite the kitchen, staying as close to the load-bearing exterior wall as possible. Distribute weight by moving one person at a time across the suspect zone. Notify IC of the compromised floor location.

options.label: B
options.text: Move quickly over the spongy section — brief contact with a weak floor is safer than prolonged weight.

options.label: C
options.text: Both crew members cross together — if the floor goes, they need each other on the same side.

node-3.title: Staircase Integrity Check
node-3.situation: Your crew reaches the staircase. The lower two steps appear charred on the underside. IC confirms the fire has been burning in the first-floor kitchen below the staircase. The treads flex when you press on them. The victim is reported on the second floor.
node-3.question: How do you assess and use the staircase?

options.label: A
options.text: Test each step with a tool before body weight. Distribute load by ascending with 1.5–2 metre spacing between crew members — never two on the same step. If any step fails, abort and use the exterior ladder as the pre-planned secondary egress.

options.label: B
options.text: Climb normally — the staircase is the fastest route and EMS needs the victim quickly.

options.label: C
options.text: Carry up a floor ladder to lay across the steps to distribute weight across multiple treads at once.

node-4.title: Victim Found — Return Path Compromised
node-4.situation: You locate the victim in a second-floor bedroom — conscious but unable to walk due to mobility impairment. While planning egress, IC radios: 'The staircase landing on the first floor has partially collapsed — do not use the stairs for egress.' The pre-planned secondary egress — an exterior ladder at the bedroom window — is in place.
node-4.question: How do you remove the victim?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the exterior ladder egress as planned. Signal the ground crew to position the ladder at the window. Lower the victim using a rescue harness or chair carry with a firefighter above for control, and guide the victim down the ladder.

options.label: B
options.text: Attempt the staircase anyway — a partial collapse may still leave a passable path.

options.label: C
options.text: Shelter in the second-floor bedroom and wait for IC to arrange interior shoring before exiting.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The victim was successfully lowered via the exterior ladder to waiting ground crew and transferred to EMS. The staircase landing failed completely moments later. Your pre-entry egress planning, floor assessment technique, load distribution on the staircase, and pre-planned ladder egress execution allowed a successful rescue while all crew members exited safely.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
